item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quite the effort! I wanted to comment first on the formatting, keeping in mind I'm using Chrome on a Windows PC. The article of the body itself is just fine, the early parts especially feeling like reading microfiche. And I love the "submit approval" doodad.

However, the right-side footnote pop-ups tended to leave graphical garbage behind when popping back in. And the hidden left-side panel, which a cool idea, moves in a herky-jerky way that rather undermines the aesthetic if you go scoping it out. Not sure if there's anything that can be done about that.

As for the story itself, I love the marriage of CotBG and AWCY. The story of how HANSARP comes together is the meat of this, and I really enjoyed the background. "Cosmic Top-Secret" is, I'm not gonna lie, pretty hokey. But from the outset, I got the sense that something was wrong, maybe the Foundation had been infiltrated or infected by something, and I though I never quite figured it out, all the talk of 'taint' and the dissolution of the Ethics Committee was massively thrilling. Also, the survey is terrifying, gj.

Thank goodness part 3 was the last real bit, though. I was beginning to agree with Dr. Lark that it was getting long.

A couple stnanks
– hide block

In offset 2, Switzerland is listed as both a primary and additional area of activity?

I didn't note the offset, but at one point, the ethics council (why council?) is said to have been "desolved". Intentional?

From offset 5: "who now a functional super-intelligence"

But if nothing else, credit must be given to the authors for writing a historical skip that takes place mostly in Germany and never once bringing up Nazis. :') +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is absolutely fantastic, this sort of dadaist nihilism is so fucking interesting. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: holy shit kaktus and woed this is phenomenal

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OK, hopefully I understand some of the themes about it.

+Spoilers
-I hope I understand this

I had to look up LHOOQ after I read the whole thing (and took a lot of notes, my ADHD was in rare form tonight). It refers to a 1919 work by Marcel Duchamp, who two years prior famously submitted a urinal he didn't make, but put his name on, to an exhibit. This was referenced with SCP-1618, "Gilded Urinal".

With L.H.O.O.Q., he had vandalized the Mona Lisa, but for him it was a "readymade". Wikipedia states that "The readymade involves taking mundane, often utilitarian objects not generally considered to be art and transforming them, by adding to them, changing them, or (as in the case of his most famous work Fountain) simply renaming and reorienting them and placing them in an appropriate setting."

As I was reading the whole thing, I was wondering why the Broken God was in this story, other than to show that the SCP was influencing members of the Church. After looking up Duchamp's work, it hit me. The Broken God inspiration was not one way, but both inspired the other.

The Church's prime motive is to search for the parts of their god and restore it to what it once was. HANSARP (either singular or plural) had something already made (the concepts of reality, fear, and cultural change), then created literal and figurative art pieces and proselytized to a group of disillusioned AWCY members to help it grow. By putting all that together he/they made a behemoth that practically caused the end of days.

With the Church, they need parts to reform the whole. With HANSARP, it's parts working in service to a whole, using the "readymade" model done by Marcel Duchamp and L.H.O.O.Q.

Does that make any sense? I've been trying to put my thoughts into words for half an hour.

Oh, also +1. It took me forever to understand it, but it was worth it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seems that the last box for offset/2 doesn't show up properly for now.

I went to the fragment page and found out that the box works well in the page, but it does not when displayed here.

EDIT : Confirmed that it shows up well again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On the initial landing page: "Full understanding of SCP-4485 is required by all foundation employees." Shouldn't Foundation be capitalized?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: how does one get the bars for the cosmic top secret? i have no idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy crap, this is great

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This will be a little longer :)

+ show block
– hide block

I love the art style, the formatting, the effort!

I like the feeling of intrigue the article causes and how the story is generally developed. Piecing the puzzle partially by myself together was really cool, though I do feel some parts were slightly rushed or if not just executed a little too fast. Basically you could have kept pulling on the fish a little longer. Again, the art style is incredible and how it ties into this cognitive anomaly that outright denies predicate logic makes it all fit perfectly well together. I also like the plot twists that basically show us the big gravity of situation. On a side note, wouldn't a completely random colour palette fit the philosophy of dadaism better than this very reddish/orange one? Nevertheless great drawing.

I think the writing style is also good. The three person interview for example was a nice addition to the whole article, though I think it would have been a better option not to directly imply that Dr. Fitzwell is also affected but rather to offer 2-3 small hints that just remind you throughout the text of that.

"…as well as adversely impact the established conventional reality as established by the SCP Foundation Overseer Council."
lmao

The last memo is great and I really do not have much to comment on it, I like the last picture, ending it in that way makes us question and fear a lot.

I have been disappointed in two ways though:

1. Document 4, "Dog Will Sniff"

Exposed subjects reported seeing duplicates of themselves, and duplicates of the duplicates within the restaurant.

This is an example where you could have pulled a very nice thing off, maybe you did and I am just stupid, but generally you could have made it so that the items themselves are described as to cause logical paradoxes or break the system of predicative logic down. The quote above led me to believe this fashion would be continued, because if a duplicate is a copy of yourself it is unnecessary to then define later that people also saw copies of copies that are copies of yourself. Even better yet you could have said the following:

Exposed subjects reported seeing duplicates of themselves, and duplicates which are duplicates only of the duplicates within the restaurant.

But that is a little too philosophical.

2. This is a very personal opinion of mine. I really do not like the trend of very cool SCP's being basically world ending. This one seems to cause a ZK class scenario in the worst case and seems to be impossible to defeat because of how quickly it spreads. I would love for there to be more balance or rather acknowledgement that yes, this SCP is very dangerous and basically takes over a whole continent, but we have ongoing plans to contain it.

What I mean to say is, I would love for the SCP to fit into the story with other SCP's. This would have caused by now probably a lot of containment breaches. But I do not count this into the review because I think it would be wrong to just say my headcanon is right and yours not.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

So since the O5 council is implied to be infected with SCP-4485-A, was the Scranton-Jates Supra-Cultural Dissociation Engine intended to look like a blender? Or am I just seeing that now that I've made that connection?

Either way, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is legitimately excellent, purely from a story and concept standpoint. If that was all this was, it would be a wholeheartedly sent +1. When viewing this from the style presentation side, it's even better. I think that the style was sort of necessary to get this across. I probably wouldn't have been as interested if this wasn't eyegrabbingly cool to look at. This is just so good. I'd give it a +2 if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hooooh, my browser was chugging on this one.

One of the most ambitious skips I've seen, and the literal only one that features AWCY? that I enjoy. Easy +1.

Edit: Only thing I didn't enjoy was the "I see you, Foundation" at the end from Hansarp. Was a bit hammy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my new favorites. My sideways eyes stuck their thumbs up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glorious. Good art, writing, story, screws, god. I imagine this is going to be presented as a term project in a FA degree, and will subsequently drive the reviewing faculty mad.

item.title: Sweet
item.content:encoded: Very cool stuff, my sideways eyes were firmly affixed to the monitor for the duration. The style trickery was a definite plus too, no technical difficulties on my end. +1

item.title: Re: Incredible
item.content:encoded: Cool, thanks for clearing that up for me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Same here. The story isn't terrible; I'm not sure I like it very much, but it probably gets a no-vote by itself.

I downvoted because of how difficult it was to actually read the damn thing. Similar gimmicks have been done before with much less disruption. And I'm not sure story needs all these flashy bits and bobs, or even wants them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Many thanks! :D I got that fixed.

We really appreciate the kind words. :]

item.title: Re: Incredible
item.content:encoded:
Thank you much!

+ Show spoilers
- Hide spoilers

The Orientation was manipulated by Dr. Lark who is in the late stages of being "infected" by HANSARP. The Foundation itself has been "infected" as well but in the opposite of direction of Dr. Lark.

The Ethics Committee was dissolved by the 05 Council as they had opposed the containment methods of 4485 (you know, tossing people into a giant capsule the moment it's suspected that they're infected.) The 05 Council, having been "infected" already and inching towards zealotry in their beliefs, wasn't happy with having their methods questions. And because the Ethics Committee questions them, they were also suspected of being "infected," so the 05 Council had them tossed in to the Scranton-Jates Engine.

item.title: Incredible
item.content:encoded:
This was a joy to read and explore. Thank you for this.

Am I correct that HANSARP manipulated the orientation so that it would infect more researchers and make sure they are approved either way?

And has the ethics committee been dissolved for true reasons or is that HANSARP as well? I could see that going either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside from all those technical difficulties some people experienced, I personally really liked the format you used for this page, and the narrative itself had a pretty good buildup. That being said… It felt like a mess to read (especially if you had to go back through all offsets everytime you clsed the page) and the climax was… Also just a buch of really long paragraphs in that style that can either really draw you in or really bore you. Well, the final offset did the latter. In my eyes it didn't really pay off. Could have been a -1, but because of the format (which I really like, congratulations) it just a novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, that JS is probably one of the things I spent the most amount of time getting to work. It seems like such a simple concept, but it took me literally days to get it to type out "THE FOUNDATION" no matter what the user typed. I'm really happy to hear it paid off!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since you've asked, I can point out one misspelling: on offset 5, under the secure progress report, the word fervor is misspelled "furvor."

That said, this SCP blew my mind and is incredibly unnerving and impressive. I have no idea how I had the presence of mind to notice a typo at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everything I love in an SCP, unnerving, strong narrative, all of it. And as a bonus I get to put my art theory class to use,

+1

item.title: Overall, the effects are great
item.content:encoded:
If I had to nitpick my issues, #1 would be that the piece overall feels verbose to an unnecessary extent. I feel like there's a lot of dead weight that could be trimmed.

#2, bit of a spag critique, on offset/5, "Objects and individuals believed to be associated with HANSARP are must be remanded to DEEPWELL-9 immediately," this line could use revision.

Additionally (edited in), on offest/5, "A cryogenic failsafe will flush the each cylinder section with liquid oxygen"

Otherwise, a very ambitious piece that paid off very well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you made my favorite artistic movement (dadaism) into an SCP.

and you made it A Thing What Could End The World. (and you managed to not shoehorn in a K-class mention when it was already obvious!)

this is the most powerful +1 i could ever give to an SCP. this is the sort of thing i want to show to my friends to introduce them to SCP. a technical feat of a format screw and something that made me stare slackjawed at my screen multiple times. easily one of the best on the site. hope you get the bugs worked out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a few Technical FAQ!

Q. Why can I just skip the whole survey and not put anything in?
A. While I wanted verisimilitude to be really strong in this, I also did not want to needlessly irritate those that may be re-reading the SCP or accidentally refresh. As such, you are not required to fill in any of the entries unless you want to. The Foundation may be keeping track, though ;]

Q. On offset/3 or offset/4, why does the "Submit for Approval" always give me an X?
A. These are not the intended way to get to the next "chapter," so to speak. I did not give people the ability to move to the next part that way as you would miss out on parts of the SCP if you did so.

For offset/3, you need to get to the end of the Survey, press "Submit," and wait for the link that brings you to the next part.

For offset/4, you need to click the right arrow until you get to the final page. It will appear to glitch and the final link will, again, appear for you to click.

Q. [Insert technical issue here]], can you fix it?
A. I'd love to! Just make sure to give me some information so I can do so. I need Device you are browsing on (e.g. Samsung Galaxy S5/iPhone 8/Windows PC/etc.), as well as the Browser and Version you are using (e.g. Firefox 68.0.2/Chrome 76.0.3809.132/etc.). For mobile devices, the phone version and the browser you are using should be enough.

Thanks guys! I hope I can get this to be a good experience for everyone. :]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edit: nvm, I missed a thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm very happy you enjoyed it! :D

though i did spot a few grammatical errors and/or word repeats

Oh! If you notice them, please let us know!

and the cohesion could use some work

I actually can't disagree with this. Working within the confines of Wikidot makes cohesion quite difficult in a storytelling method such as this. It's probably the main personal quibble I have with it.

item.title: Re: Downvote, mostly due to technical difficulties
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that was entirely my fault for some changes I made yesterday. That should be fixed now.

item.title: Re: Downvote, mostly due to technical difficulties
item.content:encoded: But since I couldn't open the survey again, I couldn't continue… :(

item.title: Re: Downvote, mostly due to technical difficulties
item.content:encoded:
I'll see about getting the font fixed. As well as those other technical issues.

1) able to not answer
2) able to fill in no answer

This was intentional. You can only go to the next part of the story by "completing" the survey. I did not wish to literally force people to actually fill it out unless they chose to.

3) able to close it on the top-right X, after which, could not open it again, only it made the X appear again…

That is odd and should not be the case… I worked really hard to make sure that wouldn't happen. :/

Actually, at this point I could no longer "click to submit approval".

Also intentional. The proper way to advance the story is via the end of the survey. If all went well, you wouldn't need to click that button at all.

item.title: Downvote, mostly due to technical difficulties
item.content:encoded:
So Here's a more thorough review:

I'm still having a ton of difficulty reading commas vs periods. This is what it looks like to me.

I had a lot of difficulty with the footnotes during the HANSARP piece. The HANSARP subtitle was out of alignment. Footnote 8 didn't appear at all upon hovering, and Footnote 7 was partially obscured.

I do not believe "AND I SAW NATIONS" by HANSARP was useful or added anything to the piece, and can be removed.

For the survey, I was:
1) able to not answer
2) able to fill in no answer
3) able to close it on the top-right X, after which, could not open it again, only it made the X appear again…

Also, is the survey page meant to have all this space at the bottom?

Actually, at this point I could no longer "click to submit approval". I'm going to downvote at this point because I cannot continue to read the article anymore. Of what I did read, I found much of it to be a lot of setup, but the setup itself wasn't extremely interesting to me. The examples of all contained articles were not that necessary to me, for example, and I found the first text format to be unpleasant to read and not entirely useful to me.

I also didn't find the notes at the top to be useful, since, for example, they claimed to be tracking my eyes even though I'm not using a machine with a camera.

EDIT: Of note I am not trying to break this to be clear. I'm just dicking around as a user does, reading stuff, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"What do you trust most" "The Foundation" gave me chills and also "Just Monica Just Monica Just Monica" vibes

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is the longest SCP i've ever read, even by 1730's standards, and this only serves to compound with the fact that the story is interesting, the premise is interesting, it makes me want to know what happens next. absolutely love it, though i did spot a few grammatical errors and/or word repeats, and the cohesion could use some work

+1

item.title: Re: Seems to be a typo, but I cant edit to fix it.
item.content:encoded:
Ah, thanks! I'll get that fixed. :]

Since it's a listpages article, you have to go to the fragments to edit things. They are all linked in the Source of the main page.

item.title: Seems to be a typo, but I cant edit to fix it.
item.content:encoded: In the initial SCP report description, the last line reads "Additional information pertaining to SCP-4485's discovery is available in Agent Norman's contained within this file." I believe it should read "Additional information pertaining to SCP-4485's discovery is available in Agent Norman's report contained within this file." Thank you for your time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hansarp moment

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The sheer ambition of this secured my upvote, and I actually found the underlying anomaly and story compelling. I agree with the post upthread that the particular brand of crazy here feels somewhat like Fifthism. The surreal nature of the quiz in particular really unsettled me. I did have a little technical trouble, as I wasn't able to click through from offset 4 after the quiz. I eventually just "cheated" by typing the URL to offset 5, which worked, but it was a bit frustrating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wow fuck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Encountered this too.
Probably because it does not work on my browser so I can not continue upon seen a picture. Pressing "next" does nothing. Thought I should type some quiz answer to continue until I realized I should use another browser.

Using a slightly older version of firefox which should be modern enough (or not)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am still rather unsure about the ending here; there's something generic about the Hansarpness in it which leaves the whole thing thematically a bit weak, with the strong aesthetic built up in the early parts of the narrative and in Woed's incredible CSS work petering out. Still, this is overall a very good skip, with Woed's magic-trickery complementing an unsettling, dark skip full of cold-war paranoia (I can't deny that the ending does that well). +1, good job both of you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If AWCY? has an Apollyon skip, does that make them cool yet?

and If they are cool, what is left for them to do?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article made me intensely uncomfortable, which I think is a credit to how successful I found it.

Not only did this article enthrall me into it's framing device and affect me emotionally in a way that most don't, I feel it elucidated some of the ideas behind Dada to a hard-science studying plebeian like myself well enough that I can understand just how clever the way this article uses Dada is. Very good. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It hasn't gone over your head, you're trying way too hard. It's just part of the narrative, it's not some secret or anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it felt like it made a little too much sense actually

Dadaist writings always made some measure of "sense." If it didn't, they'd just be writing random gibberish with no meaning. There was always something to pull out from what they were saying, even if it just boiled down to "Everything is meaningless."

I can't really comment on how you felt with the UI of particular elements. It's your taste and I don't think I can really say anything to convince you otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know, maybe the fact that this is the first skip that has actually punctured through my jaded disinterest enough to *anger* me is worth something, maybe that's the whole point of the thing?

Woo, boy, no. Not at all.

Nothing I can think of to type into the comprehension quiz is doing anything.

It's not really supposed to "do" anything, per say. It's just part of the experience and the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not referring to the Dadaist artwork, rather the buttons and interface of the assessment and documentation. I could tell it was all very well done, but I ultimately felt like I was clicking buttons in a flash game — it didn't evoke any tone of dread or of a sterile, shadowy paranormal organization, so the extra effort spent on it actually pulled me out of the story instead of increasing my immersion.

The Dadaist artwork is a separate thing — it felt like it made a little too much sense actually — but I understood what you were getting at, and the artwork was well-executed. Just not to my tastes.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: In the future, please do not reply to your own comments. Use the edit button (which I'm glad to see you've already found).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know, maybe the fact that this is the first skip that has actually punctured through my jaded disinterest enough to *anger* me is worth something, maybe that's the whole point of the thing?

If it is, I'm not impressed.

Something has gone way over my head and I can't even find it on multiple re-reads.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nothing I can think of to type into the comprehension quiz is doing anything.

I'm left with an annoyed feeling of being left out of an obvious-in-hindsight in-joke.

Actually, make that Extremely Annoyed.

I might not be the smartest person on the block, but I don't like Skips that rub my face in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
many of the extra elements just felt cartoonish.

Not sure if you're referring to the HANSARP documents, but they were all heavily inspired by Dadaist artwork. Which, if you go take a look, is quite absurd and "cartoonish."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, it certainly wasn't cheap.

Something can be very expensive and expertly crafted, and still be unsuitable for a given narrative.

I do live theater, and we scrap beautiful and costly props/costumes/set pieces all the time because they just don't serve the story as effectively as a simpler, toned-down alternative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Parts of this grabbed me but on the whole, I just didn't find it compelling. The idea of "accidentally unlocking human consciousness" is interesting but I didn't feel like the reading experience backed it up. Hiccups in the graphics & interface distracted me, rather than adding to the story — many of the extra elements just felt cartoonish. And except for the interface, the core story didn't have much to differentiate it from other "failed quarantine" stories.

I'm sure this was a ton of work to put together and I can admire the skill involved, but the storytelling wasn't original or engrossing enough for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor point - it's 'make do', not 'make due'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ The truth at last?
- The truth at last?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
just a cheap way

Oh, it certainly wasn't cheap. Considering how much time it took Woed to develop, and what the going rate is for people in that profession, it's certainly an expensive investment.

cram more stuff in.

I obviously can't tell you what to think but take my word for it that this isn't the case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm personally not a fan of this new object classification system with the whole risk class and disruption class thing. I get that it's not a thing you have to do, but I feel like it's just a cheap way to cram more stuff in.

That being said, I do like this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When logged out, the message from Lark on offset 3 and 4 are missing.

Otherwise, love this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know why, but I think this format is becoming the new meta.

1
